friction_items.description: 198+ unique tools in TOOL_DEFINITIONS (claim of 344 includes aliases/handlers). A fresh AI agent has to scan an enormous catalog to pick the right call. For a close-reading scenario only ~12 tools are ever needed (sys_boot, tool_route, sys_protocol_get, sys_file_write/read, tool_web_scrape, tool_humanities_essay_scaffold, tool_synthesize, tool_paper_compile_typst, tool_audit_quality_full, mem_log, tool_ground). The other 180+ are noise during routing.
friction_items.suggested_fix: Phase 9 consolidation: collapse routing trio (tool_route + tool_quick_route + tool_semantic_route) into one tool with mode=; collapse 18 figure/dashboard tools into 3-4; collapse 8 search providers into one (already done as tool_search but the deprecated aliases still appear in TOOL_DEFINITIONS and inflate the catalog).

friction_items.description: Scenario explicitly names Project Gutenberg as the corpus, but tool_humanities_archive_lookup covers internet_archive/hathitrust/dpla/europeana/gallica/loc only — NO Gutenberg. A naive AI looking at the archive list will either invent a Gutenberg branch (silent failure) or fall back to generic tool_web_scrape (no provenance, breaks the source_provenance protocol downstream).
friction_items.suggested_fix: Add 'gutenberg' to the archives enum in tool_humanities_archive_lookup with endpoint https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/search/?query={q} and a note on the plain-text mirror at /files/. Bonus: detect Gutenberg URLs in tool_context_intake to auto-route.

friction_items.description: close_reading.yaml step `declare_step_contract` instructs the AI to call `tool_humanities_apparatus_audit` if it is 'available (planned; see docs/ROADMAP.md)'. The tool DOES NOT EXIST in TOOL_DEFINITIONS or _HANDLERS. A naive AI will attempt to call it, get a tool-not-found error, and have no recovery path. The phrase 'planned' inside a shipped protocol description is technical debt.
friction_items.suggested_fix: Either (a) implement tool_humanities_apparatus_audit before v2.0, or (b) strip the reference from the protocol and replace with the concrete fallback ('manually review against quality_bar' is already there but is buried under the planned-tool sentence). Option (a) is the right call given humanities is in-tree.

friction_items.description: tool_audit_synthesis default-denies when zero PDFs are present across literature-required steps. Humanities essays cite books, archival mss, and critical editions — many citations have no PDF (or the PDF is a Google Books snippet). A naive AI will hit BLOCK at synthesis time, have to discover override_no_pdfs=true + override_rationale, and learn this only after a frustrating cycle. The gate is implicitly a STEM-PDF assumption shipped to humanities.
friction_items.suggested_fix: Have tool_audit_synthesis check researcher_config.domain. If domain=='humanities', either skip the no-PDF default-deny or replace it with 'no recoverable citation anchor' (which the source_provenance protocol already produces). Same fix likely needed for theory_math.

friction_items.description: tool_audit_step_completeness requires (b) at least one focal figure under outputs/figures/ AND (c) sibling .caption.md + .summary.md. Close reading produces NO figures by design. The protocol tries to escape via step_summary.yaml.step_intent=apparatus + figure_required=false, but the AI has to know to write that file BEFORE the audit fires. If the AI runs tool_audit_quality_full first (a natural exploration), it sees a BLOCK referencing 'missing focal figure' with no hint that step_intent=apparatus would have waived it.
friction_items.suggested_fix: When the step has no figures AND no step_summary.yaml, the audit should emit a SOFT warning suggesting `set step_intent=apparatus to waive figure-gate` (with a concrete one-line example) rather than a hard BLOCK. Or auto-detect step_intent from the protocol_id that owns the step (close_reading => apparatus).

friction_items.description: close_reading.yaml is 234 lines, 7 steps, each step description is 8-15 lines of literary-critical prose (New Critics, Empson's seventh ambiguity, Jakobson's 'set toward the message', etc.). Gorgeous for human readers; for an AI parsing 'what is the next action' it forces extra cognition to extract the executable kernel. Multiply by 118 protocols and the AI is burning tokens on philosophy not work.
friction_items.suggested_fix: Add a `concise_steps:` block to every protocol with imperative action verbs ('1. Pick passage. 2. Pin edition. 3. Annotate line-by-line. 4. Write apparatus.'). Keep the rich prose for human reading but let the AI consume the concise form. Could be auto-generated from steps[].name + steps[].id.

friction_items.description: Three router tools exist: tool_route, tool_quick_route, tool_semantic_route — plus sys_semantic_tool_search. The naive AI doesn't know to call tool_quick_route FIRST (the description says 'Call before tool_route on every prompt') unless it has read the description for each. sys_help only tells the AI about tool_route. Layering is implicit.
friction_items.suggested_fix: Either fold quick-route detection into tool_route itself (return is_quick=true when it matches), or have sys_boot's response include 'first call: tool_quick_route, then tool_route'.

friction_items.description: Humanities pack has 3 tools (archive_lookup, transcribe, citation_chain) but 8 protocols. close_reading, distant_reading, hermeneutic_method, digital_humanities_workflow, scholarly_edition all have NO pack-specific tool. The AI is expected to write everything as raw markdown via sys_file_write — which works, but means the pack's actual leverage is thin compared to the wet_lab or engineering packs.
friction_items.suggested_fix: At minimum add: tool_humanities_annotation_scaffold (writes the annotation file template with line-anchor columns), tool_humanities_apparatus_audit (already promised), tool_humanities_collation_build (for scholarly_edition). Doesn't have to be the full tool — scaffolding the file is the 80%.

friction_items.description: close_reading.next_protocol = humanities/citation/citation_chains. But the scenario goal is the humanities_essay output, which lives at synthesis/humanities_essay_structure. The chain forces the AI through citation_chains FIRST, which is correct for honest scholarship but adds at least one extra protocol traversal the AI may not realise is mandatory. The protocol description hints at it ('After essay assembly, route to synthesis_paper for compile') but the routing graph itself doesn't make the close_reading -> citation_chains -> humanities_essay_structure -> synthesis_paper chain obvious in one place.
friction_items.suggested_fix: Add a `chain_preview` block to the close_reading router entry showing the typical 3-4 protocol path so the AI can plan the whole arc on first router call. Or have tool_route return a recommended_chain alongside primary_protocol.

friction_items.description: Protocol references `templates/step_summary.yaml.template` and `analysis_plan.yaml classify_step_intent`. The naive AI has no built-in way to enumerate templates; it has to know to call sys_file_list on templates/ — or trust the path exists. No tool returns 'show me all templates the protocols can ask me to use'.
friction_items.suggested_fix: Add sys_templates_list (or fold into sys_help topic='templates') that returns the catalog of templates + a one-line purpose per template. Tiny addition, big orientation win.

friction_items.description: detect_humanities runs from tool_intake_autofill 'when the core detector is uncertain'. For a naive AI that hasn't dropped files yet (turn 1), the detector doesn't fire — so even though researcher said 'Whitman close reading', the AI may not know to flip researcher_config.domain=humanities until later. The Gutenberg-specific terms (gutenberg, .txt etext) aren't in the term list.
friction_items.suggested_fix: Have tool_route inspect the prompt for humanities trigger phrases and propose researcher_config.domain=humanities when matched, with a one-call sys_config_set suggestion. Add 'gutenberg', 'etext', 'public domain text' to _HUMANITIES_TERMS.

friction_items.description: tool_search consolidated the 5 provider-specific tools, but the 5 old names (tool_search_arxiv, tool_search_crossref, tool_search_pubmed, tool_search_semantic_scholar, tool_search_web) still appear in TOOL_DEFINITIONS as deprecated aliases. They show up in tool listings and inflate the perceived catalog size for a naive AI doing sys_active_tools.
friction_items.suggested_fix: For v2.0.0 (MAJOR), drop the deprecated aliases from TOOL_DEFINITIONS (keep in _ALIASES only). Same for tool_plan_{turn,advance,clear}, tool_grounding_register, tool_ground_from_context, tool_claim_verify. Use the MAJOR bump to clear the alias debt.

recommendations: Phase 9 consolidation should target the figure/dashboard cluster (18 -> 4) and the audit cluster (24 -> ~10) FIRST — these are the biggest noise sources for non-empirical packs.
Implement tool_humanities_apparatus_audit before v2.0.0 ships, OR delete the dangling reference from close_reading.yaml. Shipping a 'planned' tool reference in a stable protocol is a v2.0 blocker.
Add Gutenberg as a first-class archive source in tool_humanities_archive_lookup — the validation scenario is the canonical humanities corpus and the pack should not miss it.

Make audit gates domain-aware: tool_audit_synthesis no-PDF default-deny should not fire for domain=humanities (and likely not for theory_math either). Same for tool_audit_step_completeness figure-gate when step_intent=apparatus is implicit from the protocol_id.
Drop deprecated aliases from TOOL_DEFINITIONS (keep in _ALIASES) — v2.0.0 MAJOR is the right window to clear the surface and shed ~15-20 tools from the visible catalog.
Add concise_steps: to every protocol YAML for AI consumption; keep rich description for human reading. ~10 lines per protocol, biggest single token reduction available.
Fold tool_quick_route detection into tool_route so the AI does not have to know about the two-step ritual. sys_boot's hint about the call order is too easy to miss.

Add chain_preview / recommended_chain to router output so the AI can plan multi-protocol arcs (close_reading -> citation_chains -> humanities_essay_structure -> synthesis_paper) in one shot rather than discovering each next_protocol incrementally.
